title: AI Agents
description: Build AI systems that plan, use tools, and execute safely through controlled workflows.
outcomes: Design agent goals, tools, and permission boundaries.
Build memory strategies for continuing multi-turn agent work.
Plan and govern multi-step workflows with fail-safes.
Handle human-in-the-loop review for risky operations.
Measure and improve agent reliability under failure conditions.

lessons.title: What is an Agent?
lessons.description: Differentiate passive tools from autonomous planning systems.
checklist: I can explain the difference between tool use and agent planning.
I can identify tasks that benefit from autonomy.
I can describe one agent contract and boundary.
questions: When is an agent overkill versus simple prompt chaining?
Why do product constraints matter before adding autonomy?

lessons.title: Tools
lessons.description: Build safe tool surfaces for agents with typed contracts.
checklist: I can map each tool to explicit input/output contracts.
I can limit side effects by tool policy.
I can validate tool calls before execution.
questions: How do strict schemas reduce wrong tool calls?
What is a minimal safe tool for dangerous operations?

lessons.title: Memory
lessons.description: Preserve context, state, and checkpoints for agent continuity.
checklist: I can describe short-term and persistent memory.
I can prune stale context and noise.
I can persist checkpoints for handoff.
questions: What should an agent remember across sessions?
How do you avoid stale memory causing bad decisions?

lessons.title: Planning
lessons.description: Decompose goals into ordered plans that can execute safely.
checklist: I can design a simple plan as ordered steps.
I can add explicit stopping criteria.
I can recover from a partial or failed plan.
questions: How does planning reduce repeated retries?
When should an agent stop and ask for help?

lessons.title: Multi-Step Workflows
lessons.description: Chain multiple tools while preserving context and control.
checklist: I can connect tool outputs to later steps.
I can add branching logic for alternate paths.
I can detect loops and prevent infinite execution.
questions: What is the first sign of an execution loop?
Why is step-level observability essential in multi-step flows?

lessons.title: Human Approval
lessons.description: Introduce guardrails where human confirmation is required.
checklist: I can define high-risk actions that require review.
I can design clear approval payloads.
I can preserve context across approval handoffs.
questions: Which actions should never be fully autonomous?
How do approvals improve trust without blocking everything?

lessons.title: Agent Failure Modes
lessons.description: Detect hallucinations, stalling, drift, and unsafe tool use.
checklist: I can name common failure patterns.
I can add fail-closed policies.
I can define retry and escalation rules.
questions: Why does confidence reporting without policy cause risk?
How do you prevent silent failure masking?

lessons.title: Agent Evaluation
lessons.description: Measure end-to-end success, latency, cost, and safety outcomes.
checklist: I can set success criteria beyond token correctness.
I can track agent completion and escalation rates.
I can evaluate cost-to-value trends.
questions: How do completion metrics differ from satisfaction metrics?
Which dashboard fields catch failures early?

lessons.title: Mini Project: Job Application Assistant Agent
lessons.description: Create an assisted workflow that drafts and reviews applications with approval.
checklist: I can plan and execute a multi-step workflow.
I can add a safe handoff for human review.
I can log every tool action.
questions: How do you define user trust checkpoints?
What failures should trigger human review first?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: Agent for Job Applications
lessons.description: Build a portfolio-grade agent that sources roles, drafts responses, and tracks approvals.
checklist: I can ship a full agent flow with checkpoints.
I can manage memory and state across turns.
I can document governance and escalation behavior.
questions: How do you balance speed, quality, and control?
What risk metrics would you report weekly?

lessons.title: AI Agents Quiz
lessons.description: Validate reliability-first agent design before production adoption.
checklist: I can explain the core safety contract of agents.
I can identify and mitigate failure modes.
I can propose measurable controls.
questions: How should an agent respond to uncertain outcomes?
What would you include in an agent safety policy?

quizQuestions.prompt: Explain in your own words what makes an AI agent different from a simple tool-calling prompt.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: An AI agent plans and executes multi-step actions over time with memory and state, while tool calling is usually a single-step invocation under explicit user or system control.

options.label: Multi-step goals with tools
options.explanation: An AI agent plans and executes multi-step actions over time with memory and state, while tool calling is usually a single-step invocation under explicit user or system control.

options.label: Broad unconstrained tools
options.explanation: Agents need scoped tools, strict schemas, and permission boundaries to avoid unsafe actions.

options.label: Permanent memory
options.explanation: Agent memory can become stale, private, irrelevant, or misleading without retention rules.

options.label: No human review
options.explanation: Human approval is important when actions are costly, irreversible, sensitive, or risky.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why must every agent tool expose a strict schema and safety policy?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Strict schemas define allowed inputs and outputs, while safety policy limits damage and prevents unsafe or unintended tool operations.

options.label: Permanent memory
options.explanation: Agent memory can become stale, private, irrelevant, or misleading without retention rules.

options.label: Strict schemas and safety policies
options.explanation: Strict schemas define allowed inputs and outputs, while safety policy limits damage and prevents unsafe or unintended tool operations.

options.label: No human review
options.explanation: Human approval is important when actions are costly, irreversible, sensitive, or risky.

options.label: Just a longer prompt
options.explanation: Agents coordinate goals, steps, tools, state, and guardrails beyond a single response.

quizQuestions.prompt: What memory mistakes are common in agents and how do they affect outcomes?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Noisy or stale memory leads to repeated work, wrong assumptions, or unsafe decisions, while proper pruning and checkpointing preserve context quality.

options.label: No human review
options.explanation: Human approval is important when actions are costly, irreversible, sensitive, or risky.

options.label: Just a longer prompt
options.explanation: Agents coordinate goals, steps, tools, state, and guardrails beyond a single response.

options.label: Unlimited retries
options.explanation: Retries need limits and fallback handling to avoid loops, costs, and unintended side effects.

options.label: Stale or noisy memory
options.explanation: Noisy or stale memory leads to repeated work, wrong assumptions, or unsafe decisions, while proper pruning and checkpointing preserve context quality.

quizQuestions.prompt: Describe when a human-in-the-loop step should be mandatory.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Human review is needed for high-risk changes, financial commitments, irreversible actions, or any decision with compliance implications.

options.label: Review for high-risk actions
options.explanation: Human review is needed for high-risk changes, financial commitments, irreversible actions, or any decision with compliance implications.

options.label: Just a longer prompt
options.explanation: Agents coordinate goals, steps, tools, state, and guardrails beyond a single response.

options.label: Unlimited retries
options.explanation: Retries need limits and fallback handling to avoid loops, costs, and unintended side effects.

options.label: Hidden tool behavior
options.explanation: Logs are needed to debug decisions, failures, permissions, and outcomes.
